title: Hannah Muniz -Services Hiring Manager Interview | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Hannah Muniz -Services Hiring Manager Interview | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: Hello.
Hello.
Sorry, just one moment.
No, you're totally fine.
Rig set up here.
I am just enjoying my coffee, so no rush.
Okay.
Cool.
Yeah, I just poured a cup, too.
I wish I could say it was my first, but unfortunately it's a little deeper in this stimulant stack than I'd like to be at this point in the morning.
Yeah.
Let's see.
Let's Let's see.
you.
Okay.
Signs of life.
There we are.
Okay.
Hi.
How are you?
Hi.
Good.
Nice to meet you.
I'm Hannah.
Nice to meet you too.
Yeah.
My name is Matthew.
I'm the chief content officer here at GrowthX.
My job is basically all related to kind of the quality of output.
Obviously at this point, that's client deliverables, but in the larger picture, it's how well does our product work?
You know, how, how closely does it hew to our ideal expectations of output?
What it feels like in terms of structure and tone, repeatability, all of that stuff.
Consistency, guess you'd call it.
So all of that thing, all of that, that kind of stuff.
And that is, that is where my focus is a large portion of the time.
And of course I work closely with all the people that operate the tools here internally to create client work, to keep an eye on their feedback.
Because they are our customers, you know, they're using the product every day, making work for clients, that sort of thing.

And then if at some point, you know, we decide to release that product publicly, all of that will have benefited us as we won't have to rely on the long tail, you know, getting feedback from customers, which is, it's always harder.
So, yeah, it's a lot more nebulous than, you know, having the in-house staff working on it and frankly complaining about it.
And I'm like, please complain.
The more you complain, the better it gets.
But yeah, that's kind my role here.
And I'm happy to kind of talk to you a little bit, hear a little bit about you and kind of where you're at.
So you don't have to read the whole LinkedIn or anything, but just give me a little intro and kind of where you've been and what you've been up to.
Yeah, yeah.
Well, I'm Hannah.
Again, it's great to meet you.
I have about 10 years of experience working in digital content, mostly SEO content.
So lots of experience in that sector.
So.
Started as a freelance writer, then kind of moved my way into content management, editorial.
Most recently, I was a managing editor for a large digital marketing company called Red Ventures.
Huge company, obviously very different from a startup.
So I managed some of their higher education websites.
So most of my experience has kind of been in that higher ed space.
And yeah, we worked with a lot of different types of content.
I did have a team of about four to six editors and writers.
It changed a lot, obviously, because you just kind of have to learn to pivot with big corporations.
know, there's a lot of reorgs and strategy shifts.
So, but yeah, we worked with a lot of the B2C kind of marketing content on our website.
We were a lead generating company.
So that's mainly how we made our money.
But also worked on content that, you know, KPIs were increasing brand awareness and just earning sessions and page views.
So a lot of different.
experience in that space.
But yeah, we worked with AI a little bit too, just to give you a little bit of a brief on that.
But yeah, so we had a proprietary AI tool at my last company that me and one other managing editor were put in charge of to kind of roll it out to our team and to figure out the best way to use it.
So yeah, that was a great experience.
I think I've learned a lot about AI, so happy to talk more about that.
Yeah, I am interested.
I mean, obviously, that's our bread and butter here.
We have a set of tools that leverage AI quite a bit.
It's not quite as simple as dropping things into an assistant box or something like that, but it is definitely leveraging the tools.
So the more you can tell me about that kind of experience, the better, because it'll help me to understand, hey, you know, this is something that would feel organic to you stepping into this world.
Because as you know, you know, the content world is like bifurcated theologically at the moment about...
You know, what the future of AI is, what the future of their own jobs are, what the jobs to be done for humans are versus what the jobs to be done for LLMs are or other AI tools.
So kind of curious a little bit about your experience using that.
I think that would be a great place to start.
Yeah, for sure.
So it was about a year, a year and a half of trying to kind of get used to this new tool.
So the tool was kind of similar to, it had kind of a chat GPT element.
So it did generate content, you know, if we were to prompt engineer and feed it information that we wanted.
But it had a lot of other aspects to it, too.
was kind of like an editorial platform, we called it.
So it had things like you could put in like a topic, you know, just a simple tentative title of your article, and it would generate research for you and an outline or spec, we called it.
And then you could kind of manually, you know, get in there and change things as needed.
And it was still kind of like in the beta testing phase, though.
It was very, very new to our company.
And so we were kind of the guinea pigs with it.
And obviously, that being said, it.
You know, it was a lot of feedback being given from us to the engineering, data science team, and they were great to work with.
I think the thing was it was a tool created by engineers, but not, you by content people, so they didn't quite understand our needs.
But it ended up being a really great relationship because we just, like, had a lot of great back and forth, and they were very receptive to, you know, our ideas.
So, yeah, so we tried it to generate or to write content at first.
That was just kind of the most simple thing to do.
Okay, let's see how it does with writing our content.
You know, it was very hit or miss.
I mean, it's similar to, you know, ChatGPT.
It's not always going to be exactly how you want it.
It's going to sound a little AIE.
So there was a lot of human intervention to make it, you know, better and to really match our tone of voice and our brand.
It did have trouble, too, like, finding right, like, authoritative sources to use in our content.
It wasn't really great about sourcing information, especially data.
So that was a big side of it that we were concerned about.
But, yeah, so we...
We tried that out.
We tested that with about maybe 150 or so articles we generated through it, but it was, like I said, it really needed a little more work on that side.
And the company at the time was kind of taking a different direction.
So we moved away from the generative AI side and then started leaning into it as more of a replacement platform for Google Docs, which we've been using.
So it had a lot of tools that were, you know, AI driven, but then some other ones that didn't really use AI too.

So other things that had were like, had like a score it would give you based on different SEO features to see, you know, how well your article would do on Google and other search engines and AI tone detector, all of that.
So, so we, yeah, we tried, we tested a lot of that out.
Like I said, we were the guinea pigs.
It was fun to, you know, kind of get our toes wet there.
But, you know, my, my direct reports too, it was sometimes it was a struggle and, you we had to figure out, like I said, what kind of feedback to give them.
And how often to give them feedback and there were always bugs we had to report, of course.
So, yeah, it was a great.
learning experience.
Do you have any specific questions about the tool or anything else I can elaborate on for you?
Yeah, no, that makes sense.
I mean, think that a lot of people are kind of taking various stabs at this.
And so it's not surprising to hear, you know, the kind of rough structure that it took and experience with it.
I think like it'll be helpful to dovetail the standard kind of things that we're asking folks with.
Honestly, you've had more practical experience with it or with attempting to utilize the tools like this.
I think a lot of people.
So that's good.
So let me ask you in the context of that, like explain to me a kind of as a part of that process, you had to give a lot of feedback.
So talk to me about the way you give feedback, you know, how you were kind of walking through somebody.
Feel free to put it in the context of that AI tool where you were going through that process or another context is fine, too.
I don't want to pigeonhole you, but it seems germane.
So just kidding.
No, no worries.
I'll try to keep it short.
But in terms of the AI tool.
Yeah.
So we have like an AI, we have kind of like a liaison.
That worked with me and that other managing editor.
And then he also went back to that AI data science team.
So that was our main point person that we would talk to when we had concerns or issues with the tool.
And so it wasn't super, super like fleshed out in terms of like, okay, you have to give feedback every two weeks or anything.
But so we kind of did it on more of an as needed basis.
But I would talk, I checked in with my, we were a remote company, I should say too.
So I would check in with my direct reports, you know, every week we'd have a one-to-one.
And I'd ask about the tool, were there any pain points, you know, that were really bothering them.
We also had a running Google Doc to just, you know, track what was going on, any major issues or flaws we were finding with the tool that were really preventing us from doing our work efficiently.
And so I would have this kind of rough running doc from my team and myself as well adding to it.
And then we would, I would take some time, like maybe once or twice a month, look through it.
I would kind of collect and, you know, collate all that feedback into them.
More readable doc that, you know, kind of summarized the main issues we were having, you know, with examples.
And then I would give that to our point person and kind of spell out, okay, here are the higher priority things that we really need fixed or changed.
And then here are kind of the lower priority, nice to haves.
So that worked well for us as a team.
If there were any immediate bugs or glitches, we could just report that directly in the platform, which was great, too, because sometimes things just completely broke, you know, for no reason.
And so we, like, had to stop our work.
And so that was more like the immediate side of it.
But long-term feedback, we just kind of had an ongoing, you know, discussion over Slack and on Zoom to make sure we were all on the same page with that.
Did you use a team filing system there or did you just usually use Slack or what was your general process for reporting, like, bugs or issues?
We had an ongoing, we had a recurring meeting on Zoom.
So that was one way that I would bring up issues.
I think it was once a week or every other week.
But we would bring up any major pain points at that meeting.
And then I would send more information, you know, through a doc.
Whatever, just to kind of, you know, detail what was going on.
But the bug system or the glitch, reporting a glitch system was directly in the platform.
was a little button you could click and it would send it directly to the engineering team and you could mark, you know, high priority or low priority.
So that was better for like the bigger issues that needed immediate action.
Yeah, that sounds good.
Makes sense.
Yeah, I would venture to say there would be some similar work here.
You know, the work of getting the work done with the tools is one side of the work, but the other side of the work is, hey, we're building this.
You know, it's in process, right?
So we definitely take, I mean, I mean, it's, you know, the world that I live in, I really want to know exactly how it's working for people.
And I think it's important to, to be good at and be, I don't know, a little bit of a, a cheerleader for people's psychological state when it comes to.
of course.
Yeah.
Tools in progress, because like, hey, I have empathy for you.
We're all trying to make this thing work.
And it is working in some ways and not in others.
But we also have to get work done.
So, you know, let me be of service to you.
Like, but in order to do that, I need to know, you know, I need to know what's going on.

So talk to me a little bit about like, your directs and the people that were actually utilizing the platform, and how you kind of encourage them to keep open comms and help you to understand what was going on so that you could, you know, clear the road for them.
Yeah, no, yeah, for sure.
I, I like to think I'm a very empathetic leader, you know, so because like, I care about my direct reports.
And I, I don't, we're all remote, but like, everybody has stuff going on in their lives.
And so I was always, I made sure to check in, like I said, we had a, usually a once a week, just check in.
And that would really be for the direct, my direct report to kind of run it.
Like, you know, what do you want to talk about?
How can I serve you?
And how can I help you?
So I always tried to keep it pretty open.
But yeah, when we were using that AI tool, obviously, it was a lot.
I would like to check in with that more often.
And so some people I could tell felt bad that they didn't love it, you know, at first.
And I had to remind people, too, that, like, you know, we're the guinea pigs here.
Like, this is being tested.
It's okay if it's not working, you know, 100%.
And so I think a lot of it was me just, like, reassuring people, too, that, you know, this is not going to be perfect.
You know, I do think AI is, I mean, it's the future, you know.
And so, like, I mean, I think it's going to be part of our jobs no matter what.

so, you know, trying to kind of frame it in that way, too, of like, hey, even if you're not in love with it, like, here's how, though, we can make, you know, we can get into this world of AI, but still, you know, have a voice in it and how we use it.
And we can figure out a way to make it work for us.
And so I wanted to focus on empowering them to, you know, make this tool work for them.
Instead of just being told, you know, here's how you use it.
Because I think, like I said, I just felt like the engineers, they're great people.
They didn't quite understand our process.
So, so that was one way.
And yeah, just regular checking in.
And I think the running doc that we had was really.
Helpful, too, because, you know, people, just as things came up, if they didn't feel like, you know, running to me, they could just add it to the doc.
And then I would just kind of, you know, collect that feedback anonymously, too.
I wouldn't tell people, I didn't have to tell people, you know, who put what down.
And then I could give that to, you know, our point person.
So I think that worked really well, too, for people who had a little more, like, reservations about, you know, expressing themselves.
But I always try to encourage an open culture of feedback.
And I love getting feedback myself.
So I was always telling them, too, like, hey, how can I help you?
You know, how can I do better as a manager?
So, yeah, we had really good relationships.
Good, good.
And then talk to me a little bit about, so obviously you've run teams before.
And it could be in the context of this, you know, that project or other projects.
it's limited.
But talk to me a little bit about a time you kind of, like, had to redesign or re-architect a process.
An editorial process is obviously most germane to this.
But it can be anything.
And, you know, recognizing that there was an issue and then reconfiguring it.
I would love to hear a little bit about that.
Yeah, I think that happened quite a few times with the AI tool, you know, like I said, and I'll just stick with that because it's so relevant to this, but happy to talk about other things too.
But yeah, no, that tool, like I said, we didn't have a process around it.
We were just given the tool and told to use it.
And so being the first team to really dive into it, you we had to figure out the process ourself.
And so there, you know, in the beginning, we were just trying to, like I said, generate content and do kind of like the basics that the tool had and follow the flow in the tool.
But as the more we did that, the more we realized certain things just weren't very intuitive and it just wasn't working with our overall production process.
We used Asana as a tool to track our projects.
I don't know if you're familiar with that, but and, you know, Google spreadsheets, of course, too.
So we had a lot of different things we were using to track and it was really hard to track it when we were using this AI platform.
It just wasn't all connected well.
And so, you know, I, after getting a lot of that feedback from my team to have like this.
It's not working and like, I don't know how to track this and I don't know where this goes.
I, you know, took it upon myself to kind of just like, okay, like, let me look at this whole thing as a big picture.
And then kind of wrote up like an SOP about really explaining in detail how we can use this and how, what steps to follow to make sure we're tracking it properly.
You know, people aren't losing track of their things because that happened too sometimes.
So, um, I just wrote, I really, I love writing like instructions and like organized docs.
So that was a fun thing for me to do.
But yeah, so I wrote out this whole kind of step-by-step guide and, um, obviously I got feedback from my, the co-managing editor I was working with too.
And our AI point person making sure I didn't miss anything and that, you know, it was really getting into the nitty gritty that the editors and the writers would need to know.
So, um, yeah, we worked on that and I, you know, rolled that out to my team, asked for feedback too.
And, uh, I got really good, you know, it
Very good reception for that.
So I think it helped a lot.
We had a lot fewer issues with the tracking, like I said, and just, yeah, just making sure we were all on the same page because people were doing things very differently, too.

And I know from talking to the recruiter, Marilia, I think her name is, that I think because I had asked her kind of what would have been some of the pain points that I know, like, people were doing things very differently.
She was saying, I was like, oh, my God, yes, I can relate to that.
So I think getting on the same page is very important.
So that was, I think, one way that we kind of, you know, fixed that process a bit.
Yeah, yeah, I think that's absolutely fair.
mean, you know, when you are creating new processes on the fly, people can interpret things wildly differently.
I've found this to be true in any company, but especially in a startup, you know, because people are like interpreting your feedback and instructions in various ways.
And so it's also helpful to put in that loop of like, oh, I gave you the, probably the most clear feedback I could possibly give, and it's extremely well done.
But you still interpreted it differently than I thought.
And then it's like, hey, okay, let me examine my semantics.
Maybe it's the way that I presented the information or whatever.
But at the long story short, you just need to like make sure everybody's on the same page.
No, yeah, 100%.
Yeah, no, I encourage people to leave comments on that doc I had.
And I did get people saying, hey, I don't understand this.
Or like, hey, can we clarify this?
You know, so I, it was really good for me to kind of figure out too, like, how do we break this down in a way that everybody understands it the same way?
Yeah, makes sense.
And then, do you have any questions for me?
I do have to go in a couple minutes, but I have a little- Yeah, I have a few just quick questions.
I know that a lot of folks, because, so I know the company's only been around like just under a year.
And it seems like a lot of you folks have only been here maybe four to five months.
So I'm curious what made you want to join GrowthX.
Yeah, what drew you to the company and what made you apply?
So I was talking to Marcel and Daniel, and I was trying to decide what I was going to do next.
So I was in a little bit of a transition.
I had been working at a different-
startup in a different space for about a year.
I ran TechCrunch for 10 years.
So I was the editor-in-chief of TechCrunch.
Ran a global news organization, you know, it's 100 people all around the world.
Pretty intense environment, high production.
We published 56 stories a day nonstop for a decade.
And, you know, during the Zerp era.
So very, very crazy, wild era in startups.
Yeah, that was fun.
But it also gave me a firsthand view of how many pain points were not being solved by modern CMSs, writing tools, processes.
There were plenty of stories we lost out on simply because we didn't have the person power or the architecture to compete with larger organizations.
We beat the New York Times' stories all the time.
And they have 1,500 journalists.

But there were plenty that we couldn't because we didn't have the ability to, say, process data, you know, at scale, get the sourcing or prove out our convictions against large data sets fast enough or just.
You know, with enough human labor at that point to do that thing.
And the tooling is just bad.
You know, all of journalism and all of content production, the tools are bad, bad, bad, bad, bad, all the way down.
There's not a single CMS on the planet that isn't just kind of duct taped together.
There isn't, there aren't any writing tools of any real value.
Even Grammarly is like probably the best and it's still bad.
It's means.
Yeah, was going to say.
Yeah, it's all 100 means.
So talking to Marcel and Daniel about, you know, the way that they were thinking about content.
And then I talked to them about the way that I thought about it and the way that I came into writing and journalism.
Because I didn't go to J school.
I didn't finish college.
Instead, I taught myself to write by examining kind of what was out there on the web publicly.
And back in early 2000s and kind of understanding what I would need to do in order to break into that world.
Because I was a photographer doing other things.
And I started pulling stuff apart.
And...
And analyzing it atomically.
Hey, what are the necessary story elements that need to be here?
Who would I need to talk to to get this information?
What does a solid news story look like and feel like?
Of course, doing the research, looking at giving myself a crash day school course of my own, just trying to understand what does that look like?
And then I just did it because that's what was available to me, right?
I didn't have the ability to go to college for it and to learn how to do it through the traditional method.
So I was like, hey, the internet exists.
Let's go internet this up.
And I did that.

And then I got into writing, did my own thing, got into another publication, worked my way up through that publication through various roles, and then moved over to TC and ended up taking over TC and running that for 10 years.
And that was not due to any intelligence on my part.
It's just like a willingness to be grindy about it.
And just to understand the component parts of things.
And it was only once I started editing other writers that I realized that even the ones who had gone through did it.
Digital J school or, you know, had English degrees or whatever, which TechWitch was like 50% that and then 50% like crazy smart people about all kinds of wild topics.
We had a neuroscientist.
had like, you know, all kinds of weirdos that weren't really journalists, but we could put the architecture around them to help them to get to where they needed to get.
But that was more important.
The intellectual curiosity was more important for sure.
But anyway, long story short, once I got into editing, realized like, hey, most of these people still aren't thinking about content in this atomic way.
You know, the best book editors probably are, you know, the best at this, right?
The long form people that are used to thinking things, thinking through things in terms, those terms.
Given the speed at which everybody's expected to publish on the web and, you know, all of that, you realized, hey, most of the people don't understand the architecture.
The things that they're building, you know, they're building buildings, but they don't understand why the girders are in the places that they are.

And that work of taking that, systematizing it, understanding it, and sort of building rails that would help anybody write better, or anybody produce faster, or at scale, quality at scale, that would intrigue me.
So, sorry for the long-minute answer.
No, I appreciate it.
I very much appreciate it.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No, I think that makes sense.
Is it all right if I ask you one more quick question?
Of course.
Yeah.
Would love to know, it sounds like this is a new role, the technical managing editor.
I don't know how you all are thinking about it, but how would you define success in this role?
Like, what looks like a, you know, successful person in this?
Yeah.
It's an evolution of a previous role.
So, the previous role would be managing editor, let's just call it.

And what we realized was we just needed people with more automation experience and more ability to think architecturally about the way the product works, not just people who are good at content, right?
Sorry for the air quotes, but, you know, good at content.
It's like a, it's a solid definition for a profile.
And there are plenty of people that are pretty amazing at content itself, but.
But then they don't have that extra layer of thought process about, okay, you know, how do we break down these things and articulate them in a way that can improve process?
And that is a skill of its own.
And that's why I asked you a lot of questions about feedback and, you know, I kind of want to hear about your work with automation tooling and stuff.
Because the more people we can have that have at least had some experience in it, we know this is a nascent field.
But having that content experience layered into the automation experience makes someone like you a good candidate for the role because we do need to understand how the machinery is working.
You don't need to know the code, you know, like that's not your task.
But you would need to understand the inputs and outputs, you know, what's going in, what's coming out, in which ways is it falling down in between those processes.
You get feedback and articulate it well, solicit it from people who are utilizing the tool to create content for the clients, and honestly, be able to manipulate it and use it yourself.
I am...

Personally, of the James Cameron School of Management, where I don't have to be the best person in the world at your job, but I would like to be able to do it so that I can articulate, hey, I understand where you're falling down here.
Try and focus on these areas, right?
And I think that's what we're looking for is people that have the ability to articulate from an editorial perspective.
Hey, I love this quality, be better.
That's one mode of work, just making sure the client work is actually being delivered well and that we're meeting expectations there, our own expectations primarily, but of course, then the clients.
And then on the other side, the other side of the coin saying, hey, I understand why this expectation isn't being met.
There's actually an issue here.

Maybe that's your content manager or person operating the workflow is having a heck of a time making it work right, and therefore, they are not able to spend enough time to put the polish in, you know, in that human intervention step or steps to get it to where it needs to be.
And so if that's a frustration or.
Or a roadblock or a thing that you can remove by articulating clear feedback to ENG and then the ENG team can fix that problem and then you move forward.
And then on the other side saying, well, actually, I think the tooling is working correct, but maybe you need a little process improvement here personally.
And so it's a part and parcel thing.
It has to work together.
And that's what success looks like in a role like this is the ability to juggle those things and articulate both of them.
It is a tall ask because it is a new field, but that's where we live.
So it is what it is.
Yeah, no, that makes complete sense.
And I feel like I understand.

I mean, I didn't have like, you know, years and years of experience working with ChatGPT or AI tools, but just the past year, year and a half working with it, feel like I have a pretty good sense of kind of, yeah, figuring out whether it's the tool or the person.
That was definitely something that came up a lot, too, with us.
So, yeah, no, I'm excited about AI.
I think there's so much to do here and I want to be part of the conversation.
I don't want it to just kind of like, you know, go buy me and I'm just like here still writing from scratch.
Yeah, I feel like it's here for a reason and there's so much we can do with it.
But anyway, I know you said you have to go.
So I just wanted to thank you for your time.
It was great meeting you, Matthew.
And yeah, I just, I really appreciate you taking the time to interview me.
Thanks, Hannah.
I appreciate it as well.
Have a good one.
Thank you.
You too.
Bye-bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interview with Hannah Muniz for Technical Managing Editor role at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/xXgSfoyKPsoUC35dVuuuyKVzFGqsz2i6?tab=summary&timestamp=738.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Hannah has 10 years of digital content experience, including managing editor roles and AI tool implementation](https://fathom.video/share/xXgSfoyKPsoUC35dVuuuyKVzFGqsz2i6?tab=summary&timestamp=891.0)
  - [GrowthX seeks candidates with both content expertise and ability to think architecturally about content processes](https://fathom.video/share/xXgSfoyKPsoUC35dVuuuyKVzFGqsz2i6?tab=summary&timestamp=1001.0)
  - [Success in the role requires balancing quality content production, process improvement, and clear feedback on tool functionality](https://fathom.video/share/xXgSfoyKPsoUC35dVuuuyKVzFGqsz2i6?tab=summary&timestamp=1206.0)

## Topics
